ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis (chye sim). Closest parent match is mustard greens (raw) FOODON_03301710; closest_parent match used as chye sim is a closely related Brassica species.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for chye sim / cai xin. A00LC (Cabbages and related vegetables) is the closest defensible parent-category anchor within the Brassica family.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis (chye sim) in raw form; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: Chinese mustard green leaf (Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw chye sim is approximately 85g (about 1 cup of loosely packed leaves and tender stems), as commonly used in a stir-fry or blanched vegetable side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Mustard Green Leaf (Chye Sim), Raw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, calcium, and iron sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for Chinese mustard green leaf (Chye sim), raw (cr_id F14010017). Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sugar, sodium, potassium, saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, folate, minerals) estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for mustard greens raw (FDC ID 169256) as the closest approved secondary source, with adaptation noted.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; analogous estimate for non-starchy leafy Brassica vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chye sim (Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis) is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous non-starchy leafy Brassica vegetables (e.g., cabbage, bok choy) reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 85g serving calculated as (15 × 3.01 × 0.85) / 100 = 0.38, rounded to 0.4. This is consistent with the negligible glycaemic impact expected for low-carbohydrate leafy greens.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (65mg/100g) with high potassium (330mg/100g) and calcium (143mg/100g) supporting DASH dietary targets
hypertension.guidance: Raw chye sim is highly suitable for hypertension management due to its low sodium content (65mg/100g) and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (~5:1); prepare with minimal salt and avoid oyster sauce or soy sauce to preserve the sodium benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.01g/100g), low GI (~15), and high dietary fibre (~2.5g/100g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chye sim is an excellent vegetable choice for Type 2 Diabetes management; its very low carbohydrate content, estimated GI of 15, and dietary fibre of 2.5g/100g contribute negligible glycaemic load and support postprandial glucose control when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.04g/100g), zero cholesterol, high folate (187mcg DFE/100g), and anti-inflammatory sulforaphane from cruciferous Brassica family
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chye sim supports cardiovascular health through its negligible saturated fat and cholesterol content, high folate (187mcg DFE/100g) which reduces homocysteine levels, and sulforaphane from its cruciferous Brassica classification; regular consumption as a lightly cooked vegetable is consistent with heart-protective dietary patterns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory sulforaphane, high vitamin C (89mg/100g) antioxidant support, and calcium (143mg/100g) for bone health in corticosteroid-treated patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chye sim is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its sulforaphane content (anti-inflammatory), high vitamin C (89mg/100g) for antioxidant protection, and calcium (143mg/100g) supporting bone density in patients on long-term corticosteroid therapy; lightly blanch or stir-fry to preserve bioactive compounds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese mustard green leaf, known locally in Singapore as chye sim (菜心) or cai xin, is a tender leafy Brassica vegetable (Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis) widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, and southern China. In its raw state, chye sim presents bright green, slightly glossy leaves attached to pale green tender stems, with a crisp texture and a mild, subtly sweet flavour with faint bitterness characteristic of the mustard family. It is one of the most commonly eaten leafy greens in Singaporean Chinese cuisine, typically prepared by blanching in boiling water and serving with oyster sauce and garlic oil, or stir-fried with garlic and light soy sauce. Nutritionally, raw chye sim is a low-calorie, nutrient-dense vegetable providing 24 kcal per 100g, with 2.15g protein, 0.34g fat, and 3.01g carbohydrate. It is an excellent source of calcium (143mg/100g), iron (1.73mg/100g), vitamin C (~89mg/100g), folate (~187mcg DFE/100g), and vitamin A (~148mcg RAE/100g). Its dietary fibre content (~2.5g/100g) supports digestive health and glycaemic control. As a cruciferous vegetable, chye sim contains sulforaphane, a bioactive compound with documented anti-inflammatory and potential chemoprotective properties. Its very low glycaemic index (~15) and negligible glycaemic load make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The vegetable is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is a staple component of plant-based and low-calorie dietary patterns in Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. Secondary nutrients estimated from USDA FoodData Central mustard greens raw (FDC ID 169256) as the closest approved secondary source. GI estimated from peer-reviewed analogous Brassica data (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Overall confidence is high given exact HPB primary source match and well-supported secondary nutrient estimation.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy 24 kcal, protein 2.15g, fat 0.34g, carbohydrate 3.01g, calcium 143mg, iron 1.73mg per 100g) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14010017, confidence 0.95). 2. Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sugar, sodium, potassium, saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, folate, minerals) estimated from USDA FoodData Central mustard greens raw (FDC ID 169256) as the closest approved secondary source; values are consistent with the HPB primary data profile. 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for raw leafy vegetables per USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: plant food, naturally zero cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg set to 0.0: plant food, naturally zero B12. vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: raw leafy green, negligible vitamin D per USDA FoodData Central. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy Brassica vegetables in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for chye sim exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. FoodOn match is closest_parent (FOODON_03301710 mustard greens raw); FoodEx2 match is closest_parent category-level (A00LC Cabbages and related vegetables); no SNOMED CT code identified. 6. Occasion tag 'snack' from input cultural data retained in meal_context; primary meal use is as a cooked vegetable side dish. 7. Keto dietary tag from input retained as dietary flag context; at 3.01g carbohydrate/100g, chye sim is compatible with ketogenic dietary patterns.
